content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 2, 2017

Heather M. Minner
City Attorney
Shute, Mihaly & Weinberger LLP
396 Hayes Street
San Francisco, CA 94102

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-220

Dear Ms. Minner:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Half Moon Bay Planning
Commissioner John Evans regarding his duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict
of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as
common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

May Commissioner Evans make, participate in making, and influence a decision concerning
commercial property located within 500 feet of (and across the street from) his own commercial
property?

CONCLUSION

Commissioner Evans may take part in the decisions. Based on the facts provided, it does not
appear that the decisions will impact the character of the Commissioner's neighborhood or the fair
market value of the Commissioner's property, nor will it impact the value of the Commissioner's
business or those of his commercial tenants.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-220
Page No. 2

FACTS

The City of Half Moon Bay is a city with a population of approximately 12,000 located in
coastal San Mateo County.

On October 10, 2017, the Planning Commission will decide whether to approve a Coastal
Development Permit, Architectural Review, Historic Preservation Permit, and Parking Exception to
allow the following: (1) The construction of a 290-square foot addition to the rear of a commercial
building; (2) Demolition of the existing detached garage and a 217-square foot
expansion/reconstruction of the demolished garage on a 5,000 square-foot site (collectively the
"Project").

The property is located at 630 Purissima Street and contains a residential building converted
for commercial use. The property is zoned C-D, Commercial Downtown and is included in the
City's historic resource inventory. The property is currently occupied by an interior design firm.
The Project site currently contains one garage parking space and several uncovered spaces in
tandem within the existing driveway. The site is considered legal non-conforming as it relates to the
off-street parking required for the existing business.

A new accessible parking space would be installed within the driveway, and the existing
garage would be replaced and enlarged to allow for two vehicle parking stalls in tandem. The
tandem garage parking would not satisfy commercial off-street parking, however. Under existing
regulations, four nontandem off-street parking spaces would be required. The Project would provide
two garage spaces and one accessible space in tandem. Thus, a parking exception is required for
three parking spaces. However, you noted that there is almost always sufficient parking along this
block of Purissima Street to serve existing uses.

The proposed addition would provide additional office space for the modestly sized
commercial office space (872 square feet existing/290 square foot addition). A new accessible
bathroom, parking space and exterior lift would be constructed. A new pervious paver concrete
driveway would be installed between the expanded garage and sidewalk/driveway apron. The
building addition and expanded garage would be consistent with the design of the existing garage
and building. The materials include composition shingles and siding that historically compatible
with that of the existing building.

Commissioner Evans' has an ownership interest in real property located across the street
from (and within 500 feet of) the Project site at 635 Purissima Street. The Commissioner's property
is a mixed-use commercial investment property. Commissioner Evans' business, an architecture
practice, occupies a portion of the commercial building. The building also includes four other
offices and two residential studio apartments, all of which are leased out to other tenants. The
commercial tenants are: a hair salon (one owner operator), a chess gaming consultant (one owner
operator), a business consultant (one owner operator and one employee), an acupuncturist (one
owner operator with single clients at one time), and a commercial videographer (one owner & one
employee).

File No. A-17-220
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of the Act if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's interests as specified in Section 87103. Based on your facts, the pertinent interests
in Section 87103 are:

• A business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more
(Section 87103(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) Commissioner Evans has an interest
in his architectural business and an interest in the rental of the mixed-use property.

• Real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section
87103(b).) Commissioner Evans has an interest in his residence and the mixed-use property.

• A source of income, including promised income, aggregating $500 or more within 12
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) Commissioner Evans has an interest in his
tenants in the mixed-use property as sources of income.²

Foreseeability and Materiality

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a
named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's
agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) However, the interests you have identified are not explicitly
involved in the decisions. Accordingly, a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can
be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. A financial effect
need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) Different
standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be
material depending on the nature of the interest. (Regulation 18702.) The materiality standard for
any particular interest are provided in Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.5.

Real Property

A foreseeable financial effect on real property, not explicitly involved in the decision, is
material if the decision meets any of the materiality standards in Regulation 18702.2(a)(7)-(12).³
The materiality standards most pertinent to the Commissioner's circumstances are the following:

² Commissioner Evans also has an interest in his clients from his architectural business from whom he received
$500 or more in the past 12 months. You have not asked about, nor provided facts related to any clients. Therefore, we
have not analyzed this potential basis for conflict of interest.

³ Regulation 18702.2(a)(11) provides a test for materiality that impacts property that is within 500 feet of the
property line of the official's real property. However, that provision further states that if the property is commercial (as
here) materiality is analyzed under Regulation 18702.1.

File No. A-17-220
Page No. 4

"(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of
property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise
levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the
market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial
interest;

* * *

"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision
was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the
market value of the official's property."

In your letter, you stated that although the Commissioner's property is immediately across
the street from the Project, you do not believe that it is reasonably foreseeable that there will be a
financial effect on the property's value for the following reasons:

1. The additions and expansions at the Project site occur in the rear of the property and are
not visible from Commissioner Evans' Property.

2. Traffic and Intensity of Use: You also stated that the minor additions were not expected to
alter traffic levels or intensity of use of the Project site. Moreover, Commissioner Evans' Property
includes sufficient parking for the businesses and residents that are located there (parking is
provided per zoning code requirements: 11 parking spaces for 2,700 square feet of commercial
space and two 1-car garages for two studio apartments).

3. Parking: The studio apartments on the Commissioner's Property have assigned parking
and their use will not change.

4. Character of the Neighborhood: An Historic Resource Evaluation was prepared by a
qualified architectural historian. The report concludes that the proposed project, including the
parking configuration and rebuilt garage, would contribute to the historic integrity of the resource
on the subject property. The proposed addition and rebuilt/expanded garage has been designed to
match the existing residence in terms of roof slope, colors, and materials in conformance with the
recommendations of the City's Residential Design Guidelines. Because of the relatively low height
of the addition (12 ½ feet), small size and location in the rear of the site, the addition will not affect
the street side appearance of the building. The project designer worked closely with an architectural
historian to ensure that the proposed changes contribute to the historical significance of the
property.

Considering that the Project site will continue to be used for the same purposes and impacts
beyond the site will be minimal, it does not appear that the decision will impact the character or fair
market value of the Commissioner's property.

File No. A-17-220
Page No. 5

Individuals who are Sources of Income

The Commissioner also has various interests in business entities, both by virtue of his
ownership interest and his receipt of income. With respect to sources of income, who are
individuals not explicitly involved in the decision, Regulation 18702.3(a)(2) provides that a
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a decision on the interest is material if the individual,
"will be financially affected under the standards applied to an official in Regulation 18702.5, or the
official knows or has reason to know that the individual has an interest in a business entity or real
property that will be financially affected under the standards applied to a business interest in
Regulation 18702.1 or 18702.2, respectively."4 With respect to the residential tenants who are
individuals, you have provided no facts to indicate that it is foreseeable there will be a measurable
financial effect on the residential tenant's personal finances (see Regulation 18702.5), or that the
residential tenants' business entities or property interests will be materially affected (see Regulation
18702.1 and 18702.2).5

Thus, the tenants would not be a basis for disqualification and we do not further analyze the
effects on the residential tenants.

The Commissioner's Businesses and Business Tenants

The materiality of a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest in a business entity
including a source of income that is a business entity, is analyzed under Regulation 18702.1. (See
Regulation 18702.3(a)(3).) For interests such as the Commissioner's businesses and business
tenants that are not explicitly involved in the decision, a financial effect is material if a prudent
person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's
financial effect would contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded
stock, or the value of a privately-held business entity. (Regulation 18702.1(b)).6

You stated that you believe that the businesses will not be materially affected. You noted the
following:

1. Commissioner Evans' Property includes sufficient parking for the businesses and
residents that are located there. Parking is provided per the zoning code and require 11 parking
spaces for 2,700 square feet of commercial space. As such, the businesses on the Commissioner's
property do not rely on street parking and any change in street parking that did occur would not

4 Regulation 18702.5 states that a financial effect on an official's personal finances is material if the official or
member of the official's immediate family, "will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision."

5 The reasonably foreseeable financial effects of a governmental decision on any real property in which a
person has a leasehold interest is material if the decision will: (1) change the termination date of the lease; (2) increase
or decrease the potential rental value of the property; (3) increase or decrease the rental value of the property, and
(4) enable a right to sublease the property; (4) change the official's actual or legally allowable use of the real property;
or (5) impact the official's use and enjoyment of the real property.

6 For example, this standard may be met if the decision was to make improvements in the surrounding
neighborhood such as redevelopment projects, traffic road improvements, or parking changes that may affect the
amount of business the business entity receives.

File No. A-17-220
Page No. 6

impact visitors to any of the businesses. In addition, there is almost always sufficient parking along
this block of Purissima Street to serve existing uses.

2. The interior design firm does not compete with the architectural firm. Moreover, the
expansion and upgrades to the design firm's building do not increase the interior design firm's
business and the 290 square-foot addition of commercial office space is not expected to increase
traffic at the site.

Based on the facts, it does not appear that the Project will impact the value of the
Commissioner's business or the businesses of his commercial tenants.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack Woodside
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division
